enrichment.short_description: tldraw is an open-source infinite canvas SDK and whiteboard app for React that lets developers embed drawing, diagramming, and multiplayer collaboration tools into their own products.
enrichment.seo_title: tldraw Review 2026: Pricing, Features and SDK Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how tldraw's infinite canvas whiteboard and React SDK work, what it costs, and how it compares for developers building drawing tools.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: tldraw is made by tldraw Inc, a London-based company founded by Steve Ruiz in 2022, building on an open source side project Ruiz started in 2021. The company offers two things under one brand: a free web app at tldraw.com for sketching, wireframing, and visual brainstorming, and the tldraw SDK, a React library that gives developers a production-ready infinite canvas editor to embed directly into their own products. The SDK is what sets tldraw apart from typical whiteboard tools, packaging the same rendering engine, shape system, and multiplayer sync layer that power tldraw.com into a library other companies can drop into their own interfaces. It underlies canvas features in ClickUp Whiteboards, Padlet Sandboxes, and Jam's screenshot annotation tool, plus experimental AI demos like make-real, which turns hand-drawn wireframes into working code. tldraw raised a 10 million dollar Series A in April 2025 led by Lux Capital and Definition Capital, with Amplify Partners also participating, bringing total funding to roughly 12 million dollars; the company says it closed just under 1 million dollars in SDK license deals in its first year of commercial operation. The consumer app is free with no paid tier. Commercial SDK use requires a license: free for development, hobby projects, and a 100-day trial, then 6,000 dollars per year (about 500 dollars per month) per team for production use, a flat fee introduced with SDK v4.0 that sparked debate over affordability for small startups. tldraw competes with Excalidraw, Miro's embeddable tooling, and Figma's canvas features, but its differentiator is developer embeddability rather than a hosted app alone.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is tldraw
paragraphs: tldraw is an infinite canvas whiteboard and SDK made by tldraw Inc, a London startup founded by Steve Ruiz in 2022 that grew out of an open source side project Ruiz began building in 2021. The company offers two things under one brand: a free consumer-facing whiteboard app at tldraw.com used by individuals and small teams for sketching, wireframing, and visual brainstorming, and the tldraw SDK, a React library that gives developers a production-ready infinite canvas editor they can embed directly into their own products.

The SDK is what sets tldraw apart from most whiteboard tools. Instead of only offering a hosted app, tldraw packages the same rendering engine, shape system, and multiplayer sync layer that power tldraw.com into a library other companies can drop into their own interfaces. This has made it the underlying canvas technology behind features like ClickUp Whiteboards, Padlet Sandboxes, and Jam's screenshot annotation tool, as well as countless internal tools and AI demo projects such as the viral make-real experiment that turns hand-drawn wireframes into working code.

tldraw raised a 10 million dollar Series A in April 2025 led by Lux Capital and Definition Capital, with Amplify Partners also participating, bringing total funding to roughly 12 million dollars. The company has said it closed just under 1 million dollars in SDK license deals in its first year of commercial operation, and it continues to develop both the free whiteboard app and the licensed SDK in parallel.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core of tldraw is its infinite, zoomable canvas with a full shape library, including sticky notes, arrows, geometric shapes, freehand drawing, text, images, embeds, and frames, all rendered with the same tldraw shape engine whether you're using the hosted app or a custom embed built on the SDK. Multiplayer collaboration is built in, letting multiple people draw on the same canvas in real time with visible cursors and presence, and the app supports exporting boards as SVG, PNG, or JSON.

For developers, the SDK exposes a full API for defining custom shapes, tools, and UI, along with a pluggable multiplayer backend so teams can wire tldraw into their own auth and storage systems rather than depending on tldraw's hosted infrastructure. This customizability is why product teams choose it over building a canvas from scratch or licensing a closed source whiteboard engine.

tldraw also powers a set of experimental and AI-connected micro-products, including tools that convert freehand sketches into working UI code, which showcase how the canvas can serve as an input surface for AI agents and generative design workflows, not just static diagramming.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The tldraw.com whiteboard app is free to use with no paid consumer tier. For commercial use of the tldraw SDK in a shipped product, companies need a license: development and hobby projects can use the SDK for free, and there is a 100-day free trial for evaluating commercial use, after which a paid commercial license is required. The standard commercial license is priced at 6,000 dollars per year (roughly 500 dollars a month) per team, a flat fee introduced with SDK version 4.0 that sparked debate in the developer community over affordability for small startups.

tldraw has indicated it is open to extended trials and different terms for pre-revenue startups and companies working with incubators, and larger organizations with custom deployment or support needs can contact tldraw directly for enterprise terms. There is no self-serve tiered pricing beyond the flat commercial license at this time.

features.title: Infinite Canvas
features.description: Pan and zoom across an unbounded whiteboard surface for sketches, diagrams, and wireframes.

features.title: Real-Time Multiplayer
features.description: Multiple users can draw and edit the same board simultaneously with live cursors and presence.

features.title: Full Shape Library
features.description: Built-in shapes including sticky notes, arrows, text, freehand drawing, images, embeds, and frames.

features.title: React SDK
features.description: An embeddable, source-available React library for building custom canvas experiences inside other products.

features.title: Custom Shapes and Tools
features.description: Developers can define their own shape types, tools, and UI panels on top of the core engine.

features.title: Export Options
features.description: Boards export to SVG, PNG, or JSON for use elsewhere.

features.title: AI and Code Generation Demos
features.description: Experimental features like make-real turn hand-drawn wireframes into working code.

features.title: Self-Hostable Multiplayer Backend
features.description: Teams can plug the SDK into their own storage and authentication instead of tldraw's hosted servers.

pros: Free and full-featured hosted whiteboard app for individuals and small teams
Highly customizable SDK that lets developers embed a canvas rather than build one from scratch
Proven at scale, powering canvas features in products like ClickUp and Padlet
Active open-source-rooted community and fast-moving engineering team
Real-time multiplayer collaboration built in from the start
cons: Commercial SDK license costs 6,000 dollars a year, a steep jump for early-stage startups
The consumer app lacks advanced project management or presentation features found in tools like Miro
SDK licensing terms and thresholds have changed over time, creating some uncertainty for adopters
No native mobile app beyond the responsive web experience
Enterprise-level support and custom terms require direct sales contact rather than self-serve checkout

faqs.question: Is tldraw free to use?
faqs.answer: The tldraw.com whiteboard app is free for anyone to use. The tldraw SDK is free for development, hobby projects, and a 100-day trial, but commercial production use requires a paid license.

faqs.question: How much does the tldraw SDK cost?
faqs.answer: The standard commercial license for the tldraw SDK costs 6,000 dollars per year, or about 500 dollars per month, per team.

faqs.question: Who makes tldraw?
faqs.answer: tldraw is made by tldraw Inc, a London-based company founded by Steve Ruiz in 2022, building on an open source project Ruiz started in 2021.

faqs.question: What is the tldraw SDK used for?
faqs.answer: The SDK lets developers embed a full infinite canvas editor, including shapes, tools, and multiplayer sync, directly into their own web applications rather than building a canvas engine from scratch.

faqs.question: What products use the tldraw SDK?
faqs.answer: Known adopters include ClickUp Whiteboards, Padlet Sandboxes, and Jam's screenshot annotation feature, along with many smaller tools and internal products.

faqs.question: Does tldraw support real-time collaboration?
faqs.answer: Yes, both the hosted app and SDK support real-time multiplayer editing with live cursors and shared presence.

faqs.question: Is tldraw open source?
faqs.answer: tldraw's core began as an open source project, and parts of the codebase remain publicly viewable, but commercial production use of the SDK now requires a paid license under its source-available terms.

faqs.question: How is tldraw different from Miro or Figma?
faqs.answer: Unlike Miro or Figma, which are primarily hosted apps, tldraw's main differentiator is its embeddable SDK, letting other companies build their own branded whiteboard features on top of tldraw's canvas engine.

use_cases.title: Embedding a Whiteboard in a SaaS Product
use_cases.description: Product teams use the tldraw SDK to add native drawing and diagramming boards inside their own app, such as project management or design tools, without building a canvas engine from scratch.
use_cases.audience: Software engineering and product teams at SaaS companies

use_cases.title: Team Brainstorming and Wireframing
use_cases.description: Designers and product managers use the free tldraw.com app for live sketching sessions, user flow diagrams, and quick wireframes during meetings.
use_cases.audience: Designers, product managers, and startup teams

use_cases.title: AI Agent and Code Generation Demos
use_cases.description: Developers use tldraw's canvas as an input surface for AI experiments that turn hand-drawn sketches into working interfaces or diagrams into structured data.
use_cases.audience: AI researchers and developers building generative design tools

features: Unlimited use of the tldraw.com whiteboard
Full shape and drawing tool library
Export to SVG, PNG, or JSON
Full access to the tldraw SDK for evaluation
Free for development and hobby projects indefinitely
Production use of the tldraw SDK in a commercial product

enrichment.short_description: TMetric is a time tracking and productivity app made by Devart that helps freelancers and teams log work hours, manage projects and budgets, and generate client-ready timesheets and invoices.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (up to 2 seats); paid plans from $7/user/month
enrichment.seo_title: TMetric Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: TMetric time tracking software: pricing plans, key features, pros and cons, and who it's best for in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: TMetric is a time tracking, timesheet, and productivity management application made by Devart, a Czech Republic-based database and developer tools company founded in 1997. TMetric began in 2016 as an internal productivity tool at Devart before being released publicly as a standalone product. It offers browser extensions, desktop apps for Windows, macOS and Linux, and mobile apps, letting individuals and teams track time against clients and projects, monitor budgets, capture screenshots and app or site activity, run payroll-ready reports, and sync with calendars. Pricing follows a freemium model: a Free plan supports up to two seats with unlimited time tracking, clients and projects, and reports; the Professional plan (around 7 dollars per user per month) adds billable rates, budgets, invoicing, and task management; the Business plan (around 9 dollars per user per month) adds screenshots, activity monitoring, time-off tracking, and integrations with QuickBooks, Jira, Redmine and ClickUp; and an Enterprise tier offers custom pricing, secure deployment, and volume discounts. TMetric integrates as a timer overlay inside more than 50 popular web apps, making it a lightweight alternative to standalone tools like Toggl Track, Time Doctor, or Harvest for teams that want tracking embedded directly into their existing project management tools. It targets freelancers, agencies, and small-to-midsize teams who need billing-grade time data without the overhead of enterprise workforce management software.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is TMetric
paragraphs: TMetric is a time tracking and timesheet application developed by Devart, a software company based in Prague, Czech Republic that has built database and developer tools since 1997. TMetric itself started life in 2016 as an internal tool Devart used to track its own team's productivity before the company decided to package it as a standalone product for external customers.

The tool is aimed at freelancers, agencies, and small-to-midsize teams that need to track billable hours, manage project budgets, and produce client-ready reports and invoices without adopting a heavier enterprise workforce management suite. It works as a lightweight timer that can be launched from inside more than 50 popular web apps, in addition to its own web dashboard, browser extensions, and native desktop and mobile apps.

Because Devart's core business is developer and database tooling, TMetric has a reputation among reviewers for being a stable, no-frills utility rather than a flashy product, with a straightforward feature set focused on accurate time capture, budgeting, and reporting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: TMetric's central feature is a start and stop timer that can be triggered from its own apps or embedded directly inside other web tools such as project trackers and helpdesks, so users don't have to switch context to log time against a task.

On the management side, TMetric offers project and client budgets with alerts when a project approaches its budget limit, billable and non-billable rate configuration, timesheet approvals, and client-ready invoicing. Team plans add screenshot capture and activity monitoring, app and website usage tracking, and a time-off module for tracking vacation and leave alongside worked hours.

TMetric connects to calendar apps for automatic time entry suggestions, and integrates with tools like Jira, Redmine, ClickUp, and QuickBooks so time data can flow into existing project management and accounting workflows rather than living in a silo.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: TMetric uses a four-tier freemium model. The Free plan supports up to two seats and includes unlimited time tracking, unlimited clients and projects, exportable reports, and API access, making it usable for solo freelancers at no cost. The Professional plan, priced at roughly 7 dollars per user per month, adds billable rates and expenses, project budgets and alerts, time rounding, client invoicing, and calendar sync.

The Business plan, around 9 dollars per user per month and marked as TMetric's most popular tier, layers on the time-off module, team timesheets, screenshot capture, activity monitoring, and deeper integrations with tools like QuickBooks, Jira, and ClickUp. An Enterprise plan offers custom pricing with volume discounts, secure deployment options, and priority support for larger organizations. Annual billing on paid plans saves up to 20 percent compared to monthly billing.

features.description: Start and stop time tracking from inside more than 50 popular web apps without leaving your workflow.

features.description: Set budgets per project or client and get notified as spending approaches the limit.

features.title: Calendar Sync
features.description: Import calendar events as suggested time entries to reduce manual logging.

features.title: Time-Off Management
features.description: Track vacation, sick leave, and other time off alongside worked hours on the Business plan.

features.title: Reporting and Exports
features.description: Generate and export detailed timesheet and productivity reports for payroll or billing.

features.title: Third-Party Integrations
features.description: Native integrations with Jira, Redmine, ClickUp, and QuickBooks.

Affordable per-seat pricing compared to many competing time trackers
Timer can be embedded inside dozens of other web apps for frictionless tracking
Strong budgeting and invoicing tools for freelancers and agencies
Backed by Devart, an established developer tools company with decades of experience

Fewer built-in integrations than larger time tracking platforms
Mobile app functionality is more limited than the desktop and web experience

faqs.question: Is TMetric free?
faqs.answer: Yes, TMetric offers a free plan for up to two seats with unlimited time tracking, clients, and projects, though advanced features like budgets and screenshots require a paid plan.

faqs.question: Who makes TMetric?
faqs.answer: TMetric is made by Devart, a software company based in Prague, Czech Republic, that has built developer and database tools since 1997.

faqs.question: How much does TMetric cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at roughly 7 dollars per user per month for the Professional tier, with the Business tier around 9 dollars per user per month; annual billing saves up to 20 percent.

faqs.question: Does TMetric track screenshots?
faqs.answer: Screenshot capture and activity monitoring are available on the Business plan, not the Free or Professional tiers.

faqs.question: Can TMetric generate invoices?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Professional plan and above let you convert tracked billable hours into client invoices.

faqs.question: Does TMetric integrate with other tools?
faqs.answer: TMetric integrates with project and accounting tools including Jira, Redmine, ClickUp, and QuickBooks, and its timer can run inside more than 50 other web apps.

faqs.question: What platforms does TMetric support?
faqs.answer: TMetric offers a web dashboard, browser extensions, desktop apps for Windows, macOS, and Linux, and mobile apps for iOS and Android.

faqs.question: Is TMetric good for freelancers?
faqs.answer: Yes, many freelancers use the free plan or Professional tier for tracking billable hours, managing project budgets, and invoicing clients.

use_cases.title: Freelance Time and Billing Tracking
use_cases.description: Freelancers track billable hours across multiple clients, set project budgets, and generate invoices directly from logged time.

use_cases.title: Agency Team Time Management
use_cases.description: Agencies use screenshots, activity monitoring, and team timesheets to track how staff time is spent across client projects.

use_cases.title: Embedded Tracking Inside Existing Tools
use_cases.description: Teams that already use Jira, ClickUp, or Redmine add TMetric's timer directly inside those apps to log time without switching tools.
use_cases.audience: Software and project teams using existing PM tools

pricing_plans.billing_cycle: Monthly or annual (annual saves up to 20%)
Monthly or annual (annual saves up to 20%)

enrichment.short_description: Toast is a cloud-based, Android-powered point-of-sale and restaurant management platform covering ordering, payments, payroll, and guest engagement for restaurants of all sizes.
enrichment.seo_title: Toast POS Review 2026: Pricing, Features for Restaurants
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Toast restaurant POS: pricing plans, key features, pros and cons, and how it compares for restaurants in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Toast, Inc. is a cloud-based restaurant technology company founded in 2011 in Boston, Massachusetts by former Endeca colleagues Steve Fredette, Aman Narang, and Jonathan Grimm. The company built an Android-based point-of-sale system specifically for the restaurant industry, later expanding into a full platform covering online ordering, delivery, payroll and team management, inventory, guest loyalty and marketing, and integrated payment processing. Toast went public on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker TOST in September 2021 at 40 dollars per share, and as of 2026 the company employs roughly 5,700 people and serves well over 100,000 restaurant locations across the US and expanding international markets, with quarterly annualized recurring revenue surpassing 2 billion dollars. Pricing starts with a free Starter Kit tier for one or two terminals that carries higher payment processing rates, moving up to a Point of Sale plan starting around 69 dollars per month with lower processing rates, and custom enterprise pricing for multi-location or complex operations that need online ordering, payroll, and advanced inventory. Hardware, installation, and PCI compliance fees typically add several hundred to a few thousand dollars to a restaurant's real monthly cost. Toast competes with Square for Restaurants, Clover, TouchBistro, and Lightspeed Restaurant, differentiating itself through restaurant-specific workflows, an integrated payments business, and an expanding suite of add-on modules like Toast Tables for reservations and waitlist management.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Toast
paragraphs: Toast is a restaurant technology company founded in 2011 in Boston, Massachusetts by Steve Fredette, Aman Narang, and Jonathan Grimm, three former colleagues from the enterprise software company Endeca. The founders set out to build a point-of-sale system designed specifically for the operational realities of restaurants, running on Android hardware rather than the iPad-based systems that dominated the market at the time.

What began as a POS terminal has grown into a full restaurant operating platform that spans front-of-house ordering, payment processing, back-of-house kitchen display systems, online ordering and delivery, payroll and team scheduling, inventory management, and guest marketing and loyalty tools. Toast went public on the New York Stock Exchange in September 2021 under the ticker TOST, and by 2026 the company reports roughly 5,700 employees and quarterly annualized recurring revenue above 2 billion dollars, with well over 100,000 restaurant locations using the platform.

Toast's business model combines software subscriptions with integrated payment processing, meaning the company earns revenue both from monthly platform fees and from a cut of every card transaction processed through its terminals, a structure similar to competitors like Square and Clover but built around restaurant-specific workflows like table management, tip pooling, and menu modifiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, Toast provides an Android-based point-of-sale terminal built for the pace of restaurant service, with menu and modifier management, split checks, tableside ordering on handheld devices, and kitchen display systems that route orders to the right station in real time. Integrated payment processing handles card, contactless, and gift card transactions directly through Toast's own hardware.

Beyond the core POS, Toast offers online ordering and delivery management, payroll and team scheduling tools, inventory tracking with vendor and recipe costing, and guest-facing loyalty and marketing modules that let restaurants build repeat business through email, SMS, and gift card campaigns. These modules can be added incrementally as a restaurant's needs grow, rather than requiring a full platform switch.

Toast also supports reservation and waitlist management through its Toast Tables product, and provides real-time reporting and analytics dashboards accessible from Toast Web, letting owners and managers track sales, labor costs, and guest data across single or multiple locations from any browser.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Toast's pricing has several tiers. The Starter Kit is a free entry point for restaurants running one or two terminals, bundling core POS, payments, order and table management, and reporting, but it comes with higher payment processing rates around 3.09 percent plus 15 cents per transaction. The standard Point of Sale plan starts around 69 dollars per month and unlocks custom hardware configurations along with reduced processing rates near 2.49 percent plus 15 cents for in-person transactions.

For restaurants that need online ordering and delivery, payroll and scheduling, automated inventory, or guest marketing and loyalty, Toast offers a custom Build Your Own plan priced through a sales quote. Real-world monthly costs typically run higher than the advertised base price once hardware (799 to 1,500-plus dollars), installation fees (250-plus dollars), a roughly 9.95 dollar monthly PCI compliance fee, and payment processing are factored in, which reviewers note can add 500 to 2,000-plus dollars a month depending on transaction volume.

features.title: Inventory and Recipe Costing

features.description: Centralized, real-time sales and labor reporting across one or many restaurant locations via Toast Web.

Wide range of add-on modules covering payroll, marketing, and reservations under one platform
Strong market presence with well over 100,000 restaurant locations and a public track record
Reduced payment processing rates available on paid plans compared to the free tier
Real-time, cloud-based reporting accessible from any browser
cons: Advertised low starting price does not include hardware, installation, and processing fees, which can add significant monthly cost
Locked into Toast's own payment processing, limiting flexibility to shop for cheaper rates
Long-term contracts and early termination fees have been a common complaint in reviews
Full feature set including payroll, marketing, and inventory requires custom pricing not published upfront
Some smaller or very simple operations may find the platform more than they need

faqs.question: How much does Toast POS cost?
faqs.answer: Toast offers a free Starter Kit for one or two terminals with higher processing rates, a Point of Sale plan starting around 69 dollars per month with lower processing rates, and custom enterprise pricing for full-featured deployments.

faqs.question: Who founded Toast?
faqs.answer: Toast was founded in 2011 in Boston, Massachusetts by Steve Fredette, Aman Narang, and Jonathan Grimm, former colleagues from the enterprise software company Endeca.

faqs.question: Is Toast a public company?
faqs.answer: Yes, Toast, Inc. went public on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker TOST in September 2021.

faqs.question: What operating system does Toast POS run on?
faqs.answer: Toast POS terminals run on Android, distinguishing it from iPad-based competitors like Square for Restaurants.

faqs.question: Does Toast handle payroll?
faqs.answer: Yes, Toast offers payroll and team scheduling as an add-on module alongside its core POS and payments platform.

faqs.question: Does Toast charge payment processing fees?
faqs.answer: Yes, Toast requires restaurants to use its integrated payment processing, with rates that vary by plan, generally lower on paid tiers than the free Starter Kit.

faqs.question: How many restaurants use Toast?
faqs.answer: As of 2026, Toast reports well over 100,000 restaurant locations using its platform in the US and internationally.

faqs.question: What is Toast Tables?
faqs.answer: Toast Tables is Toast's reservation and waitlist management add-on, sold separately from the core POS platform.

enrichment.short_description: Toast Tables is Toast's reservation, waitlist, and table management add-on that helps restaurants seat guests, manage floor plans, and sync bookings with their Toast POS.
enrichment.seo_title: Toast Tables Review 2026: Reservation and Waitlist Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Toast Tables reservation and waitlist software: pricing, features, and how it fits into the Toast restaurant platform.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Toast Tables is a restaurant reservation, waitlist, and table management product built by Toast, Inc., the Boston-based restaurant technology company founded in 2011 by Steve Fredette, Aman Narang, and Jonathan Grimm that also makes the Toast point-of-sale platform. Toast introduced Tables in April 2023 as an integrated add-on that connects guest bookings, host workflows, floor plans, and seating data directly with a restaurant's existing Toast POS system, rather than requiring a separate reservation platform like OpenTable or Resy that has to be manually reconciled with the POS. It runs as a host-facing app on an iPad or Android tablet, syncing with Toast Web so managers can configure settings, view bookings, and run reports from a browser. Hosts can seat walk-ins, add guests to a digital waitlist, text guests when their table is ready, accept both walk-in and online reservations, and pull up guest profiles that carry visit history and preferences from Toast's broader guest engagement tools. Toast has also integrated Tables with Google, letting diners search for and book a restaurant directly from its Google Business Profile. Pricing starts at 50 dollars per month for a plan that includes unlimited waitlist management and 25 reservations, with a Toast Tables Plus tier at 199 dollars per month offering unlimited reservations and waitlist together. Toast Tables is only available to restaurants already using or adopting the Toast POS ecosystem, positioning it as a bundled alternative to standalone reservation platforms such as OpenTable, Resy, or Tock for operators who want their front-of-house data unified with their point-of-sale system.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Toast Tables
paragraphs: Toast Tables is a reservation, waitlist, and table management product built by Toast, Inc., the restaurant technology company behind the Toast point-of-sale platform. Toast introduced Tables in April 2023 to give restaurants an alternative to running a separate, disconnected reservation system alongside their POS, instead syncing guest bookings, host workflows, and seating data directly with Toast's existing platform.

The product runs primarily as a host-facing app on an iPad or Android tablet at the host stand, paired with management and reporting tools accessible from Toast Web on any browser. Because it shares data with the rest of the Toast ecosystem, a restaurant's guest profiles, visit history, and loyalty information can follow a diner from the reservation stage through seating and the final check.

Toast Tables competes directly with dedicated reservation platforms like OpenTable, Resy, and Tock, but its key differentiator is that it is built into the same system restaurants already use to run payments and orders, rather than requiring operators to reconcile bookings between two separate vendors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Toast Tables lets host staff manage a live floor plan, seat walk-ins, add parties to a digital waitlist, and notify guests by text message when their table is ready. It accepts both in-person and online reservations, syncing all bookings into a single view so hosts always know what's coming next.

Guest profiles carry information from across the Toast platform, so returning diners can be recognized and personalized service can be extended based on prior visit history, order preferences, or loyalty status tracked through Toast Marketing and Loyalty. Real-time table status updates flow back to servers and managers automatically.

Toast has expanded Tables' reach through an integration with Google, allowing guests to search for and book a table directly from a restaurant's Google Business Profile without leaving Google's search results, in addition to bookings made through Toast's own guest-facing widgets.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Toast Tables is priced separately from the core Toast POS subscription. The base plan costs 50 dollars per month and includes unlimited waitlist management plus 25 reservations per month. Restaurants that need more booking volume can upgrade to Toast Tables Plus at 199 dollars per month, which removes the reservation cap and offers unlimited waitlist and reservations together.

As with other Toast products, Toast Tables is only available to restaurants running on the Toast POS platform, so its cost is layered on top of whatever core POS and payment processing plan a restaurant already has with Toast, rather than functioning as a fully standalone product.

Built-in Google Reservations integration for extra booking visibility
Flat monthly pricing rather than per-cover or per-reservation fees
Combines waitlist and reservation management in a single host workflow
Guest profiles carry loyalty and visit history from across the Toast platform
cons: Only available to restaurants already using the Toast POS ecosystem
Reservation cap on the base plan (25 per month) may be too low for busy full-service restaurants
Less established brand recognition among diners compared to OpenTable or Resy
Additional monthly cost on top of core Toast POS and processing fees
Fewer standalone marketplace discovery features than dedicated reservation platforms

faqs.question: What is Toast Tables?
faqs.answer: Toast Tables is a reservation, waitlist, and table management add-on built by Toast, Inc. that integrates directly with a restaurant's existing Toast POS system.

faqs.question: How much does Toast Tables cost?
faqs.answer: The base plan is 50 dollars per month with unlimited waitlist and 25 reservations, while Toast Tables Plus is 199 dollars per month with unlimited waitlist and reservations.

faqs.question: Do I need Toast POS to use Toast Tables?
faqs.answer: Yes, Toast Tables is designed to work as part of the Toast platform and is available to restaurants using Toast's point-of-sale system.

faqs.question: When did Toast launch Tables?
faqs.answer: Toast introduced Tables in April 2023 as an integrated reservation and waitlist management product.

faqs.question: Can guests book a Toast Tables reservation through Google?
faqs.answer: Yes, Toast has integrated Tables with Google, letting diners search for and book directly from a restaurant's Google Business Profile.

faqs.question: What devices does Toast Tables run on?
faqs.answer: The host-facing app runs on iPad or Android tablets, with management and reporting available through Toast Web on any browser.

faqs.question: How is Toast Tables different from OpenTable or Resy?
faqs.answer: Toast Tables' main advantage is native integration with the Toast POS and guest data, while OpenTable and Resy are standalone platforms with larger independent diner marketplaces.

faqs.question: Does Toast Tables support walk-in management?
faqs.answer: Yes, hosts can seat walk-ins and add them to a digital waitlist alongside online and phone reservations.

use_cases.title: Full-Service Restaurant Reservations
use_cases.description: Full-service restaurants use Toast Tables to manage reservations, walk-ins, and a live floor plan from a single host stand app.

use_cases.title: Busy Waitlist Management
use_cases.description: High-traffic restaurants use the digital waitlist and text notifications to manage guest flow during peak hours without a paper waitlist.
use_cases.audience: High-volume restaurants and casual dining spots

use_cases.title: Unified Guest Data Across Booking and POS
use_cases.description: Restaurant groups already on Toast POS use Tables to keep reservation, order, and loyalty data unified in one guest profile.
use_cases.audience: Restaurant groups running on the Toast POS platform

enrichment.short_description: Tock is a reservation, waitlist, takeout, and events platform for restaurants, wineries, and hospitality venues, now owned by American Express.
enrichment.seo_title: Tock Review 2026: Pricing, Features for Restaurants
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Tock reservation and dining platform: pricing plans, features, and how it compares to OpenTable and Resy in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Tock is a reservation, waitlist, table management, takeout, and events platform for restaurants, wineries, hotels, and other hospitality venues. It was created in 2014 by Nick Kokonas, co-founder of Chicago's Alinea Group, who built the system to solve reservation and no-show problems he experienced running his own high-end restaurants, and Tock remains headquartered in Chicago, Illinois. The platform grew into an independent reservations company before Squarespace acquired it in 2021 for more than 400 million dollars in cash and stock to expand into hospitality commerce. In 2024, Squarespace agreed to sell Tock to American Express for roughly 400 million dollars as Amex looked to strengthen its dining and cardholder benefits ecosystem, alongside a related acquisition of the payments company Rooam; the deal closed in 2024, making Tock a unit of American Express rather than an independent or Squarespace-owned company. Tock serves several thousand restaurant, winery, and hotel customers and differentiates itself from OpenTable and Resy with a flat monthly subscription model that has no per-cover or per-reservation fee, instead charging a percentage on prepaid bookings and experiences such as prix-fixe dinners or ticketed events. Plans range from a Base tier starting around 79 dollars a month up through Essential, Plus, and Premium tiers that add lower prepayment fees, POS integrations, event and experience ticketing, and deeper guest analytics. Tock To Go, a separate takeout and delivery product, is priced at a flat 199 dollars a month plus a small percentage on orders.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Tock
paragraphs: Tock is a reservation, waitlist, and hospitality commerce platform originally created in 2014 by Nick Kokonas, co-founder of the acclaimed Chicago restaurant group Alinea. Kokonas built Tock after running into the limitations of existing reservation systems at his own restaurants, particularly around prepaid tickets for prix-fixe experiences and reducing no-shows, and the company remains headquartered in Chicago, Illinois.

Tock grew from a single-restaurant tool into a platform used by thousands of restaurants, wineries, and hotels before being acquired by Squarespace in 2021 for more than 400 million dollars as part of Squarespace's push into hospitality and commerce tools. In 2024, Squarespace agreed to sell Tock to American Express for roughly 400 million dollars, alongside Amex's acquisition of the restaurant payments company Rooam, folding Tock into American Express's broader dining and cardholder rewards strategy.

Today Tock operates as part of American Express rather than as an independent company, continuing to serve restaurants and hospitality venues with reservation, waitlist, event ticketing, and takeout tools while also connecting to Amex's card member dining benefits and offers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Tock's core reservation system lets restaurants manage table bookings, walk-in waitlists, and guest communication from a single dashboard, with the ability to sell prepaid tickets for special events, tasting menus, and prix-fixe dinners directly through the booking flow, a feature that set it apart from reservation platforms built only around standard table bookings.

The platform includes guest profile and CRM tools that track dining history, preferences, and spend across visits, along with POS and payment integrations on higher tiers so reservation and table data can sync with a restaurant's existing point-of-sale system. Tock To Go extends the platform into takeout and delivery order management.

Tock's pricing philosophy is built around a flat monthly subscription with no per-cover fee, in contrast to competitors that charge a fee for every seated diner, which the company positions as more predictable for high-volume restaurants, offset instead by a small percentage fee on prepaid bookings and ticketed experiences.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Tock offers multiple subscription tiers. The Base plan starts around 79 dollars a month with no per-cover fee, while an Essential plan runs about 199 dollars a month and includes a roughly 2 percent fee on prepayments. Higher Plus and Premium tiers add lower prepayment fees, POS integrations, premium support, event management tools, and deeper guest insights, with exact pricing available on request from Tock's sales team.

Tock also sells Tock To Go, its takeout and delivery product, separately for a flat 199 dollars a month plus roughly 2 percent on orders, or 3 percent on orders without a subscription. International pricing and enterprise contracts for hotel groups and multi-location restaurant chains are quoted individually.

features.title: Tock To Go

features.description: Tools for managing ticketed events and special dining experiences.

features.title: Flat-Fee Pricing Model
features.description: Monthly subscription with no per-cover fee, unlike many competing reservation platforms.

pros: No per-cover fee, which can be more predictable and affordable for high-volume restaurants
Strong ticketed and prepaid event functionality not matched by all competitors
Backed by American Express, bringing dining benefit integrations and financial stability
Guest CRM and history tracking built into the core platform
Tock To Go bundles takeout and delivery alongside reservations
cons: Prepayment and ticketing fees apply on top of the base monthly subscription
Smaller diner-facing discovery network compared to OpenTable
Higher-tier features like POS integration require upgrading to more expensive plans
Ownership changes from Squarespace to American Express have created some uncertainty about long-term product direction
Pricing for higher tiers is not fully published and requires a sales conversation

faqs.question: Who owns Tock?
faqs.answer: Tock is owned by American Express, which acquired it from Squarespace in a deal completed in 2024 for roughly 400 million dollars.

faqs.question: Who founded Tock?
faqs.answer: Tock was created in 2014 by Nick Kokonas, co-founder of the Chicago restaurant group Alinea.

faqs.question: How much does Tock cost?
faqs.answer: Tock's Base plan starts around 79 dollars a month, with Essential around 199 dollars a month plus a prepayment fee, and higher Plus and Premium tiers priced on request.

faqs.question: Does Tock charge a per-cover fee?
faqs.answer: No, Tock uses a flat monthly subscription model with no per-cover fee, charging instead a small percentage on prepaid bookings and ticketed events.

faqs.question: What is Tock To Go?
faqs.answer: Tock To Go is Tock's separate takeout and delivery ordering product, priced at a flat 199 dollars a month plus a percentage on orders.

faqs.question: Can Tock sell tickets for special events?
faqs.answer: Yes, Tock supports selling prepaid tickets for tasting menus, prix-fixe dinners, and other special dining experiences.

faqs.question: How is Tock different from OpenTable?
faqs.answer: Tock emphasizes flat-fee pricing and prepaid ticketed experiences, while OpenTable is built primarily around standard table reservations with a larger diner-facing discovery network.

faqs.question: Does Tock integrate with restaurant POS systems?
faqs.answer: Yes, POS integration is available on Tock's higher-tier plans.

enrichment.short_description: Todoist is a task and project management app made by Doist that helps individuals and teams organize tasks, set reminders, and collaborate using natural language quick-add and cross-platform sync.
enrichment.seo_title: Todoist Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Todoist task management app: pricing plans, key features, pros and cons, and how it compares to other to-do apps in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Todoist is a task and project management app created by Amir Salihefendic and made by Doist Inc, a fully remote company with more than 100 employees across roughly 35 countries. Todoist launched in 2007, making it one of the longest-running task management apps still actively developed, and Doist has remained a bootstrapped, profitable company that has never taken venture capital funding, also building the team messaging app Twist. Todoist lets individuals and teams capture tasks with natural language quick-add, organize them into projects with sub-tasks, labels, and filters, set recurring due dates and reminders, and collaborate with shared projects, comments, and assigned tasks. It syncs across web, desktop (Windows, macOS, Linux), and mobile (iOS, Android) apps, and connects to more than 90 third-party integrations including calendar apps, Slack, and email. As of a December 2025 pricing update, Todoist offers three tiers: a free Beginner plan capped at 5 personal projects with basic reminders and views; a Pro plan for individuals at 5 dollars per user per month billed annually (7 dollars billed monthly) with up to 300 projects, full reporting history, task duration, and custom reminders; and a Business plan for teams at 8 dollars per user per month billed annually (10 dollars billed monthly) adding shared team workspaces, up to 500 team projects, roles and permissions, and centralized billing. Todoist competes with tools like TickTick, Things, Any.do, and broader project management platforms such as Asana and ClickUp, differentiating itself through a minimalist interface, fast natural language input, and a long track record of stability and cross-platform reliability.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Todoist
paragraphs: Todoist is a task and project management app first launched in 2007 by Amir Salihefendic, making it one of the oldest still-active to-do apps on the market. It is built and maintained by Doist Inc, a fully remote company with more than 100 employees spread across roughly 35 countries, which has never taken venture capital funding and operates as a bootstrapped, profitable business.

The app is designed around fast task capture and lightweight organization: users can add tasks using natural language, for example typing something like call client next Friday at 3pm automatically sets a due date and time, organize them into projects and sub-tasks, apply labels and filters, and set recurring reminders. Todoist syncs in real time across web, desktop apps for Windows, macOS, and Linux, and mobile apps for iOS and Android.

Beyond individual task management, Todoist supports team collaboration through shared projects, task comments, assignments, and activity logs, and connects with more than 90 third-party tools including calendar apps, Slack, and email clients, positioning it as a middle ground between simple personal to-do apps and heavier project management platforms like Asana or ClickUp.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Todoist's signature feature is its natural language quick-add, which parses typed or spoken text to automatically set due dates, recurring schedules, labels, and priority levels without manual form filling. Tasks can be organized into nested projects and sections, viewed as lists, boards in a Kanban style, or calendars, and filtered using custom saved filters based on labels, due dates, or priority.

Collaboration features let users share projects with others, assign specific tasks, leave comments, and track completed work through activity history and productivity trend reports called Karma in Todoist. Business plan customers get team workspaces, shared templates across the organization, and centralized administration and billing for managing many users at once.

The app also includes an AI assistant feature called Task Assist that can help break down tasks or suggest next steps, alongside deadline tracking separate from due dates, calendar integrations for two-way sync with tools like Google Calendar, and a reminders system that can notify users by location as well as by time on mobile.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Todoist's free Beginner plan supports up to 5 personal projects, list and board layouts, a handful of filter views, one week of activity history, and basic email and calendar integration, making it usable for individuals with simple task lists at no cost. Following a pricing update in December 2025, the Pro plan for individuals costs 5 dollars per user per month billed annually, or 7 dollars per month billed month-to-month, and raises the project limit to 300, adds full reporting history, custom reminders, deadlines, task duration tracking, and unlimited use of the Ramble AI feature.

The Business plan, aimed at teams, costs 8 dollars per user per month billed annually, or 10 dollars per user per month billed monthly, plus applicable local tax. It includes all Pro features for every team member plus a shared team workspace, up to 500 team projects, team activity logs, shared templates, support for up to 1,000 team members and guests, project folders, custom roles and permissions, and centralized billing. Teams can create a workspace for free before committing to a paid Business subscription.

features.title: Natural Language Quick-Add
features.description: Type or speak a task and Todoist automatically parses due dates, recurrence, and labels.

features.title: Cross-Platform Sync
features.description: Real-time sync across web, Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android apps.

features.title: Projects, Labels, and Filters
features.description: Organize tasks with nested projects, custom labels, and saved filter views.

features.title: Multiple Views
features.description: Switch between list, board, and calendar layouts for the same tasks.

features.title: Team Collaboration
features.description: Shared projects, task assignment, comments, and team activity logs on Business plans.

features.title: Recurring Due Dates and Reminders
features.description: Set flexible recurring schedules and time- or location-based reminders.

features.title: Productivity Tracking
features.description: Karma points and trend reports show completed task history and streaks.

features.title: Third-Party Integrations
features.description: Connects with 90+ tools including Google Calendar, Slack, and email clients.

pros: Long track record of stability and active development since 2007
Fast, well-regarded natural language input for quick task capture
Generous cross-platform support across virtually every major device and OS
Bootstrapped, profitable company not dependent on investor pressure to monetize aggressively
Clean, minimalist interface that avoids feature bloat found in heavier PM tools
cons: Free plan's 5-project limit is restrictive for users managing multiple areas of life or work
Recent price increases from December 2025 raised both Pro and Business plan costs
Lacks some advanced project management features like Gantt charts and resource management found in tools like Asana or ClickUp
No built-in time tracking without third-party integrations
Business plan's per-user pricing can add up quickly for larger teams

faqs.question: Is Todoist free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Todoist offers a free Beginner plan limited to 5 personal projects, with paid Pro and Business plans unlocking more projects and features.

faqs.question: How much does Todoist Pro cost?
faqs.answer: As of the December 2025 pricing update, Todoist Pro costs 5 dollars per user per month billed annually, or 7 dollars per month billed monthly.

faqs.question: Who makes Todoist?
faqs.answer: Todoist is made by Doist Inc, a fully remote, bootstrapped company founded by Amir Salihefendic, who launched Todoist in 2007.

faqs.question: Does Todoist support teams?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Business plan adds shared team workspaces, task assignment, roles and permissions, and centralized billing for teams.

faqs.question: What platforms does Todoist support?
faqs.answer: Todoist is available on web, Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android with real-time sync across all of them.

faqs.question: Does Todoist have an AI assistant?
faqs.answer: Yes, Todoist includes a feature called Task Assist and a Ramble AI feature to help with breaking down and capturing tasks.

faqs.question: How is Todoist different from Asana or ClickUp?
faqs.answer: Todoist focuses on fast, minimalist personal and team task management, while Asana and ClickUp offer heavier project management features like Gantt charts and detailed resource planning.

faqs.question: Can I use Todoist offline?
faqs.answer: Yes, Todoist's desktop and mobile apps support offline access with changes syncing once a connection is restored.

use_cases.title: Personal Task and Errand Management
use_cases.description: Individuals use Todoist's free or Pro plan to capture daily tasks, set reminders, and organize personal projects using natural language input.

use_cases.title: Small Team Project Coordination
use_cases.description: Small teams use Todoist Business to share projects, assign tasks, and track progress across a shared workspace.

use_cases.title: Freelance Client and Project Tracking
use_cases.description: Freelancers use labels, filters, and projects to separate and prioritize work across multiple clients.

enrichment.short_description: Together AI is an AI-native cloud platform providing inference APIs, GPU clusters, and fine-tuning for open-source and frontier AI models, used by developers and enterprises building AI applications.
enrichment.seo_title: Together AI Review 2026: Pricing, Features, Funding
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Together AI's inference cloud platform: pricing, key features, funding history, and how it compares for AI developers in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Together AI is an AI-native cloud computing company founded in June 2022 by CEO Vipul Ved Prakash along with Ce Zhang, Chris Re, Percy Liang, and Tri Dao, and headquartered in San Francisco, California. Prakash previously co-founded Cloudmark, an email security company, and Topsy, a social analytics company sold to Apple in 2013; Zhang, Re, and Liang bring academic AI research backgrounds from ETH Zurich, Stanford, and the University of Chicago. Together AI's platform provides serverless inference APIs for running open-source large language models, image and video generation models, and embedding and reranking models, alongside dedicated GPU instances, on-demand and reserved GPU clusters, code execution sandboxes, and fine-tuning infrastructure for training custom models. The company has raised roughly 900 million dollars across multiple funding rounds, including a 305 million dollar Series B led by General Catalyst, and in July 2026 raised an additional 800 million dollars at an 8.3 billion dollar valuation with participation from investors including Nvidia, Vista Equity Partners, and Aramco Ventures' venture arm. Pricing is entirely usage-based: serverless model inference is billed per token with rates spanning roughly 5 cents to several dollars per million tokens depending on model size and capability, image and video generation are billed per output, GPU clusters are billed per GPU-hour ranging from roughly 4 to 8 dollars on-demand with discounts for reserved capacity, and fine-tuning is billed per token processed during training. Together AI positions itself as a lower-cost, open-model-focused alternative to closed API providers like OpenAI and Anthropic, and as an alternative cloud infrastructure provider to AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure for AI-specific workloads such as training and high-throughput inference.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Together AI
paragraphs: Together AI is an AI-native cloud computing company founded in June 2022 by CEO Vipul Ved Prakash together with Ce Zhang, Chris Re, Percy Liang, and Tri Dao, and headquartered in San Francisco, California. Prakash brought a background as a repeat founder, having previously built the email security company Cloudmark and the social analytics company Topsy, which he sold to Apple in 2013, while his co-founders contributed deep academic AI research experience from institutions including Stanford, ETH Zurich, and the University of Chicago.

The company was built around the thesis that open-source AI models, run on efficient, purpose-built infrastructure, could match or beat closed proprietary models on cost and, in many cases, performance. Rather than building its own foundation models exclusively, Together AI operates a cloud platform that hosts and serves a wide catalog of open-source and partner models, alongside the GPU infrastructure needed to train and fine-tune custom models.

Together AI has raised substantial venture funding to build out its GPU infrastructure, including a 305 million dollar Series B round led by General Catalyst, and an 800 million dollar raise in July 2026 that valued the company at 8.3 billion dollars, with backing from major investors including Nvidia, Vista Equity Partners, and Aramco Ventures.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Together AI's core product is serverless inference, a pay-per-token API for running popular open-source language models such as Llama and DeepSeek variants, plus image generation, video generation, audio transcription, and embedding and reranking models, all accessible through a unified API without managing any GPU infrastructure directly.

For teams that need more control or predictable performance, Together AI offers dedicated inference on single-tenant GPU instances, provisioned throughput for reserved capacity at a fixed rate, and on-demand or reserved GPU clusters that can be rented by the hour for custom training or high-throughput workloads, with discounts of 10 to 30 percent for longer reservation terms.

The platform also includes fine-tuning infrastructure for training custom versions of open-source models on proprietary data, a code execution sandbox for running AI-generated code safely, and managed storage, positioning Together AI as a full-stack AI development cloud rather than just an inference API.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Together AI uses entirely usage-based pricing with no flat subscription fee. Serverless chat and language model inference is billed per million input and output tokens, with rates spanning from roughly 5 cents per million tokens for the smallest open models up to several dollars per million tokens for large flagship models like DeepSeek's reasoning variants; a batch processing API offers a 50 percent discount for workloads that don't need real-time responses.

GPU compute is billed by the hour, with on-demand clusters running roughly 4 to 8 dollars per GPU-hour depending on the chip type, and dedicated single-tenant inference instances starting around 5.49 dollars per hour for H100 GPUs, with reserved capacity for 7 to 180-plus days offering meaningful discounts. Fine-tuning is billed per token processed during training, starting around 48 cents per million tokens for standard supervised fine-tuning and rising for specialized model types, with a minimum charge per job.

features.description: Single-tenant inference instances for predictable, isolated performance.

features.title: Fine-Tuning Infrastructure
features.description: Train custom versions of open-source models on proprietary data.

features.title: Batch Inference Discount

features.title: Code Execution Sandbox
features.description: Run AI-generated code safely in an isolated environment.

features.title: Managed Storage
features.description: Storage designed for AI datasets and model artifacts.

features.title: Provisioned Throughput
features.description: Reserved inference capacity billed at a fixed per-minute rate for predictable high-volume workloads.

pros: Broad catalog of open-source models accessible through one unified API
Competitive per-token pricing compared to closed proprietary model providers
Full-stack offering spanning inference, GPU rental, and fine-tuning in one platform
Backed by major investors and well-capitalized after its 2026 funding round
Batch and reserved pricing options for teams that can trade latency or flexibility for lower cost
cons: Entirely usage-based pricing can make costs harder to predict than flat subscriptions
Focused primarily on open-source models rather than offering proprietary frontier models like GPT or Claude
GPU cluster rental requires more infrastructure expertise than simpler inference-only APIs
Pricing across many model and hardware combinations can be complex to compare
Competes in a crowded field of GPU cloud and inference providers, including hyperscalers with deeper resources

faqs.question: What does Together AI do?
faqs.answer: Together AI provides an AI-native cloud platform offering pay-per-token inference APIs, GPU cluster rental, and fine-tuning infrastructure for open-source AI models.

faqs.question: Who founded Together AI?
faqs.answer: Together AI was founded in June 2022 by CEO Vipul Ved Prakash along with Ce Zhang, Chris Re, Percy Liang, and Tri Dao.

faqs.question: How much funding has Together AI raised?
faqs.answer: Together AI has raised roughly 900 million dollars across multiple rounds, including an 800 million dollar raise in July 2026 at an 8.3 billion dollar valuation.

faqs.question: How is Together AI priced?
faqs.answer: Together AI uses usage-based pricing: inference is billed per token, GPU clusters are billed per GPU-hour, and fine-tuning is billed per token processed during training.

faqs.question: Does Together AI offer a free tier?
faqs.answer: Together AI does not have a persistent free plan for production use, but it offers trial credits for new accounts to test the platform.

faqs.question: What models does Together AI support?
faqs.answer: Together AI hosts a wide catalog of open-source models including Llama and DeepSeek variants, along with image, video, audio, and embedding models.

faqs.question: How is Together AI different from OpenAI or Anthropic?
faqs.answer: Together AI focuses on hosting and serving open-source models at competitive per-token prices, rather than developing and selling access to a single proprietary closed model family.

faqs.question: Can I fine-tune models on Together AI?
faqs.answer: Yes, Together AI offers fine-tuning infrastructure billed per token processed, letting teams train custom versions of open-source models on their own data.

enrichment.short_description: Toggl Track is a time tracking app made by the bootstrapped Estonian company Toggl that helps individuals and teams log work hours, analyze productivity, and generate billing-ready reports.
enrichment.seo_title: Toggl Track Review 2026: Pricing, Features, Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Toggl Track time tracking software: pricing plans, key features, pros and cons, and how it compares in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Toggl Track is a time tracking and productivity reporting app made by Toggl OU, a fully remote, bootstrapped company founded in 2006 by Alari Aho and Krister Haav in Tallinn, Estonia. The company has never taken venture capital funding and reported roughly 32.8 million dollars in annual recurring revenue in 2024, up from 14.7 million dollars the prior year, with a team of around 146 people distributed across more than 40 countries. Toggl Track offers one-click time tracking from web, desktop (Windows, macOS, Linux), and mobile apps, plus a browser extension that adds a timer button inside more than 100 other web tools so users can track time without switching context. It targets freelancers, agencies, and teams that need to bill clients accurately, analyze project profitability, and monitor productivity trends through visual reports and dashboards. Pricing follows a four-tier model: a Free plan for individuals and small teams; a Starter plan at 9 dollars per user per month adding billable rates, project time estimates, and team collaboration; a Premium plan at 14 dollars per user per month in the first year, renewing at 18 dollars, adding profitability analysis, timesheet approvals, scheduled reports, and single sign-on; and a custom-priced Enterprise plan with dedicated support and volume discounts. Toggl Track competes with tools like TMetric, Clockify, Harvest, and Time Doctor, differentiating itself through a polished, design-forward interface, broad third-party integrations, and a long-standing reputation for reliability as one of the original standalone time tracking apps. The same parent company, under the Toggl brand, also builds Toggl Plan for project timelines and Toggl Hire for skills-based recruiting.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Toggl Track
paragraphs: Toggl Track is a time tracking and productivity reporting application built by Toggl OU, an Estonian company founded in 2006 by Alari Aho and Krister Haav in Tallinn. Toggl has remained fully bootstrapped throughout its history, never raising venture capital, and has grown into a profitable business reporting roughly 32.8 million dollars in annual recurring revenue in 2024, up sharply from 14.7 million dollars the year before.

The company operates as a fully remote team of around 146 people spread across more than 40 countries, and Toggl Track is its flagship product, alongside sibling tools Toggl Plan for project timelines and Toggl Hire for skills-based hiring assessments, all sharing the Toggl brand.

Toggl Track is aimed at freelancers, agencies, and teams that need to track billable and non-billable time accurately, understand where hours are actually being spent across projects, and turn that data into client-ready reports and invoices, without requiring the heavier setup of a full workforce management suite.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Toggl Track's core is a one-click start and stop timer available on web, desktop apps for Windows, macOS, and Linux, and mobile apps for iOS and Android, along with a widely used browser extension that adds a timer button directly inside more than 100 other web tools and project management apps so time can be logged without switching context.

Beyond basic tracking, Toggl Track offers project time estimates, billable rate configuration, and profitability analysis that shows margin by project or client, plus scheduled and customizable reports that can be exported or shared automatically with stakeholders. Timesheet approval workflows and calendar integrations help managers verify logged hours against actual meetings and events.

On higher tiers, Toggl Track adds single sign-on, integrations with tools like Jira and Salesforce, and support for multiple workspaces, making it viable for larger organizations that need centralized administration alongside the simple time-tracking experience individual users get on lower tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Toggl Track's Free plan supports a limited number of users with core time tracking on web, desktop, and mobile, more than 100 tool integrations through its browser extension, calendar integrations, and productivity reports at no cost. The Starter plan costs 9 dollars per user per month and adds billable rates, project time estimates, revenue and productivity analysis, and team collaboration features, with a 30-day free trial requiring no credit card.

The Premium plan, Toggl's best value tier, costs 14 dollars per user per month in the first year and renews at 18 dollars per user per month, adding profitability analysis, fixed-fee project support, scheduled reports, timesheet approvals, customizable reporting, and integrations with Jira and Salesforce along with single sign-on. An Enterprise plan offers custom pricing with expert onboarding, a dedicated customer success manager, tailored setup, priority support, and volume discounts, available by booking a demo.

features.title: One-Click Timer
features.description: Start and stop time tracking from web, desktop, or mobile with a single click.

features.title: Browser Extension Integration
features.description: Adds a timer button inside more than 100 other web apps and tools.

features.title: Billable Rates and Profitability Analysis
features.description: Track project margins by comparing billable rates against time spent.

features.title: Timesheet Approvals
features.description: Managers can review and approve logged time before it's billed or reported.

features.title: Scheduled and Custom Reports
features.description: Automatically generated reports that can be shared or exported on a schedule.

features.title: Calendar Integrations
features.description: Sync calendar events to verify or auto-suggest time entries.

features.title: Single Sign-On
features.description: SSO support for larger organizations on the Premium tier and above.

features.title: Multiple Workspaces
features.description: Enterprise-tier support for managing several separate teams or clients under one account.

pros: Polished, design-forward interface frequently praised in user reviews
Free plan generous enough for individuals and very small teams
Browser extension integrates with a very wide range of other tools
Bootstrapped, financially stable company with a long operating history
Strong reporting and profitability analysis features on paid tiers
cons: Premium plan price increases after the first year, from 14 to 18 dollars per user per month
Screenshot and activity monitoring features found in some competitors are not part of Toggl Track's core focus
Advanced features like SSO and profitability analysis require the higher Premium tier
Enterprise pricing is not published and requires a sales demo
Per-user pricing can become costly for larger teams compared to some flat-rate competitors

faqs.question: Is Toggl Track free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Toggl Track offers a free plan for individuals and small teams with core time tracking, though it has a limited number of users and fewer advanced features.

faqs.question: How much does Toggl Track cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at 9 dollars per user per month for Starter, with Premium at 14 dollars per user per month in the first year, 18 dollars on renewal, and custom Enterprise pricing above that.

faqs.question: Who owns Toggl Track?
faqs.answer: Toggl Track is made by Toggl OU, a bootstrapped company founded in 2006 by Alari Aho and Krister Haav in Tallinn, Estonia.

faqs.question: Does Toggl Track have a browser extension?
faqs.answer: Yes, its browser extension adds a timer button inside more than 100 other web tools so users can track time without switching apps.

faqs.question: Does Toggl Track support timesheet approvals?
faqs.answer: Yes, timesheet approval workflows are available starting on the Premium plan.

faqs.question: What other products does Toggl make?
faqs.answer: Alongside Toggl Track, the same company builds Toggl Plan for project timelines and Toggl Hire for skills-based recruiting.

faqs.question: Is Toggl Track good for freelancers?
faqs.answer: Yes, many freelancers use the Free or Starter plan to track billable hours and analyze project profitability.

faqs.question: How is Toggl Track different from TMetric or Clockify?
faqs.answer: Toggl Track is known for a more polished interface and deeper profitability reporting on its Premium tier, while competitors like TMetric and Clockify often compete more heavily on lower per-seat pricing.

use_cases.title: Freelance Billing and Profitability Tracking
use_cases.description: Freelancers track billable hours per client and use profitability analysis to see which projects are most worth their time.

use_cases.title: Agency Team Time Reporting
use_cases.description: Agencies use Toggl Track's team collaboration, timesheet approvals, and scheduled reports to manage billable hours across multiple client accounts.

use_cases.title: Enterprise Time and Productivity Analysis
use_cases.description: Larger organizations use Premium and Enterprise features like SSO, multiple workspaces, and Salesforce integration to standardize time tracking across departments.

features: Time tracking on web, desktop, mobile
